question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any reason the approved works at St Mary’s Primary School, Newtownbutler, which have been endorsed under the School Enhancement Programme in January 2019 and supported by an approved business case in January 2024, remains stalled at pre-tender stage; and (ii) when the project (a) will be released to tender; and (b) works will commence, in light of the ongoing health, safety and accommodation issues affecting pupils and staff.
answer_text: St Mary’s Primary School, Newtownbutler, was announced in January 2019 to advance in design under the second call to the School Enhancement Programme (SEP).The project is now very well advanced.  The design work for the SEP project at St Mary’s Primary School has been completed and planning permission is in place.Funding is available to progress SEP projects through the design stages to pre-tender.  A decision to release projects to construction tender will be taken on a case-by-case basis, dependent on budget availability at that time.I will continue to make the case for additional funding for education through the Executive to allow projects, such as St Mary’s Primary School, Newtownbutler, to proceed to construction.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the review of Together: Building a United Community.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any assessment his Department has made in relation to the decline in brown trout stocks in the River Finn and the Erne basin; and (ii) any actions his Department plans to take to investigate the causes and support stock recovery, given the importance of these fisheries to local angling and tourism.
answer_text: My Department keeps brown trout stocks under review through scientific monitoring. The most recent assessment is the DAERA Area Brown Trout Status Report & Research Update 2024, produced by the AgriFood and Biosciences Institute (AFBI). This report provides data from a range of monitored rivers and lakes, including those within the Erne catchment.The River Finn referenced is a transboundary tributary of Upper Lough Erne in southeast Fermanagh. My Department does not routinely survey this river and does not hold longterm stock data. A small number of fish mortality incidents have occurred on the River Finn and were investigated by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency in line with established regulatory procedures. The AFBI report indicates good juvenile trout production in rivers surveyed in the Erne catchment and a slight increase in trout abundance in Lower Lough Erne.My Department continues to support trout conservation through regional monitoring, annual juvenile surveys and protection of trout stocks and their habitat through enforcement activity.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the environmental impact of tobacco filters, including any costs to Executive departments.
answer_text: There are no costs to Executive departments as all clean-up costs of tobacco filters fall under the responsibility of local councils. My department is not aware of what these costs are.My department funds the Keep Northern Ireland Beautiful Cleaner Neighbourhoods Report which provides an annual analysis of local environmental quality across all local council areas. The 2024/25 report highlights 53% of litter is cigarette related. Figures over time have indicated a downward trend in cigarette related litter compared to 78% in 2022.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any costs associated with the disposal of tobacco filters.
answer_text: My department does not currently hold any details on the costs of street cleaning, as this responsibility rests with local councils.  However, DAERA officials are currently working to bring forward legislation to introduce mandatory labelling on tobacco products with filters, to raise awareness of environmental impact and encourage proper disposal.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the benefits of the Ireland’s Hidden Heartlands marketing campaign for Enniskillen.

To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has requested that the Western Health and Social Care Trust develop a fully costed business case for the establishment of a new Emergency General Surgery service at the South West Acute Hospital.
answer_text: The South West Acute Hospital remains an integral facility within the NI hospital network, and I share the Western Health and Social Care Trust commitment to the long-term sustainability and development of the site. The Review of General Surgery NI set out evidence-based standards for Emergency General Surgery and hospitals delivering EGS must meet these standards.  It is for the WHSCT to determine how it will configure its general surgical service to meet these standards on a safe and sustainable basis that meets the needs of their population. I would fully consider any business case that is developed. In considering any future model for general surgery across the WHSCT, the Trust must follow the appropriate processes such as – if relevant - the Guidance on the Change and Withdrawal of Services.  Any decision on a permanent change to the delivery of EGS at SWAH would require approval from my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the reason why young farmers who applied for the Young Farmers Payment in 2025 have not yet been allocated entitlements or received any payments; and (ii) when these payments will be made, given that applicants are facing financial hardship and the 2026 Farm Sustainability Payment application period begins in four weeks.
answer_text: As 2025 was the final year for new applications for the Young Farmer Payment, my Department received a significantly increased number of applications, 70% above previous years. 		Good progress has been made in processing 2025 Young Farmer applications with 60% of applications now complete, and £2.1m payments issued to date. Of the remaining cases, a significant majority have been asked to provide outstanding evidence, and applicants have been contacted directly to set out what action is needed so that their applications can be progressed.Officials are fully committed to processing all evidence in outstanding Young Farmer claims as a priority to enable notification of outcome and issuing payments to eligible businesses as soon as possible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of whether the funding provided by the British Government is sufficient to deliver stability in our public services.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps his Department takes to (i) identify; and (ii) monitor, unmet need among pupils with Autism Spectrum Disorder, including those in mainstream education.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) Graduated Response Framework (GRF) outlines the expectations for schools at Stage 1 of the Code of Practice, ensuring all mainstream settings provide high quality early intervention, assessment, planning and monitoring for children and young people with SEND. The GRF ensures that unmet need is identified early through high quality teaching, systematic observation, evidence gathering, and structured support planning within the school setting. This approach is designed to ensure that children and young people, including those under assessment for Autism or with a diagnosis of Autism, receive timely, evidence based support within their school setting. Schools should monitor pupil progress, behaviour, communication and social emotional development, to ensure that concerns around possible Autism or unmet need are captured and supported.The Local Impact Team (LIT) support model has been designed around the GRF. There is an expectation that full use is made of whole-school educational provision and Stage 1 Code of Practice supports, before submitting a Request for Involvement (RFI) from the LIT. This approach standardises early identification across mainstream schools and supports more accurate, timely recognition of Autism related needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current waiting times for autism diagnosis assessments, including (i) the number of children and young people waiting; (ii) the length of the longest wait; and (iii) any actions being taken to reduce these waiting lists, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on any progress made on the Sligo to Enniskillen Greenway.
answer_text: The design and route selection phases for the proposed 75km Sligo-Enniskillen greenway, including the 18km section between Enniskillen and Belcoo, is being led by Leitrim County Council, in partnership with Sligo County Council and Fermanagh & Omagh District Council in the North. The preferred corridor was published in October 2025 and appraisal of route options to determine a preferred route is currently ongoing. To support the design development process in the North, my Department recently issued a letter of offer for 50% funding to Fermanagh and Omagh District Council to undertake geotechnical survey work. It is anticipated that survey work will commence in Spring 2026 and complete early in 2027. I look forward to further progress being made in the coming months to advance the project through the various design phases, including further opportunities for public and stakeholder engagement before the planning phases in both jurisdictions can commence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any formal memoranda of understanding or agreements that are in place governing cross-border cooperation between emergency services, including how these arrangements operate in the event of an emergency occurring in close proximity to the border.
answer_text: Two separate Memoranda of Understanding (MoU) exist relating to cross border cooperation between the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) and the National Ambulance Service of Ireland (NAS). The first describes the process for mutual aid in managing and resourcing Emergency and Urgent 999 calls, including the process whereby emergency calls – particularly when made via the mobile phone network – can often be received by the Ambulance Emergency Operations Centre of the opposite jurisdiction. The receiving service will undertake the triaging of the call and details are then passed to the control room in the correct jurisdiction for awareness. When occurring in prose proximity to the border, the receiving service may mount the initial emergency response, particularly when their resources are physically closer to the location of the incident, and crews will adhere to the clinical protocols in use by their own service. Each service may also request support from their counterpart if their own response is likely to be delayed due to operational pressures.The second MoU describes the specific situation of provision of a cross-border response to a Major Incident and is aimed at improving communication and cooperation when dealing with larger scale incidents such as occurred at Creeslough in October 2022. Overall command will normally default to the service in the jurisdiction where the incident occurs, but initial command can be undertaken by whichever service arrives on scene first. Northern Ireland Fire & Rescue (NIFRS) have a MoU or mobilising instructions in place with each of our bordering counties. Definition of the term “Incident” varies between counties. Louth County Council Fire & Rescue Service (LCCFRS) – The term "incident" shall include all incidents involving fire, Road Traffic Collision’s (RTC) or other emergencies where NIFRS or LCCFRS personnel and equipment are deployed for the protection of life, property and health of personnel, including Firefighters involved in operations. Monaghan Fire & Rescue (MFR) – The term “incident” shall refer solely to RTC’s where NIFRS or MFR personnel and equipment are deployed for the protection of life, property and health of personnel, including firefighters involved in operations.Cavan County Fire service (CCFS) – The term “incident” shall refer solely to RTC’s where NIFRS or CCFS personnel and equipment are deployed for the protection of life, property and health of personnel, including firefighters involved in operations.Leitrim County Fire Service (LCFS) – The term “incident” shall refer solely to RTC’s where NIFRS or LCFS personnel and equipment are deployed for the protection of life, property and health of personnel, including firefighters involved in operations. Donegal County Fire Service – No MoU in place but mobilising instructions are in place that have been agreed with all stakeholders.Additional mobilising instructions are in place in other counties to help our/their respon

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any guidance provided to schools when supporting children with suspected Autism Spectrum Disorder, who do not yet have a formal diagnosis.
answer_text: With the implementation of Local IMPACT Teams (LITs), a delay in autism diagnosis should not prevent a child or young person from receiving appropriate support in school.The Education Authority has advised that LITs are committed to ensuring that all children and young people with Special Educational Needs (SEN), including Autistic children and young people, receive timely and appropriate support within their educational setting, irrespective of their position on the diagnostic pathway.LITs are designed to provide earlier, tailored, and needs-led support to schools and children, particularly at Stages 1 and 2 of the SEN Code of Practice, before statutory assessment or a formal Statement of SEN is issued.Schools are supported directly by their LITs, which provide specialist advice and support based on the needs a child or young person. The autism specialists within these teams work closely with pupils, parents and educational staff to address SEN arising from autism.Guidance provided to schools, both through the Autism/SEND Central Helpline and through frontline LIT staff and EA Autism Training, consistently emphasises the importance of a needs-based approach. Schools are advised to implement a graduated response and tailor support to the individual profile of strengths and differences demonstrated by the child or young person. This approach ensures that the level and type of support offered does not differ based on whether a formal diagnosis of autism has been made.The Middletown Centre for Autism provides free training and resources for parents/carers and schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has sought or received feedback from local councils regarding their capacity to enforce the proposed new rules outlined in the sale and supply of puppies and kittens consultation.
answer_text: My officials have engaged extensively with local councils on the proposed new rules for the sale and supply of puppies and kittens. Officials met council representatives in February and April 2025 to outline the initial proposals and obtain feedback. Councils were also encouraged to submit formal responses to the public consultation. In addition, my Permanent Secretary recently met with representatives from Solace NI and NILGA where this matter was discussed. I remain committed to working closely with local councils to ensure that the proposed new rules both strengthen animal welfare and are practical for councils to implement and enforce.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) whether his Department will introduce a formal mechanism to allow for the de-exemption of dogs that have been exempted under the XL Bully legislation but have subsequently been assessed as not meeting his Department’s own XL Bully criteria; and (ii) any guidance issued to local councils to ensure that dogs which fall outside the XL Bully definition are not subject to destruction or prohibited from rehoming.
answer_text: Dog owners here are required to specify their dog's breed when applying for a licence. Consequently, XL Bully owners had to choose the breed of their dog and hold a valid licence before applying for an Exemption Certificate. Therefore, I am not minded to bring forward a de-exemption process nor am I aware of any plans to do so in the Republic of Ireland. Councils are responsible for enforcing dangerous dogs’ legislation in accordance with their established enforcement policies. My officials continue to work closely with the Northern Ireland Dog Advisory Group, which represents dog wardens, to discuss the implementation of legislation and address any related issues that may arise.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the recent Shared Island funding towards cross-border greenways.
answer_text: Cross-border greenways present a significant opportunity to boost sustainable transport, tourism, and regional connectivity and I am delighted that 2 million Euro has been secured from the Shared Island Fund to support councils in the ongoing and future development of cross-border greenways. My Department will continue to work closely with colleagues in the Department of Transport, Transport Infrastructure Ireland and colleagues in the relevant local councils to maximise this funding opportunity and support them in the delivery of their ambitions for active travel.Allocation of funding is subject to public expenditure appraisal processes, including approval of project business cases. When the scoping and appraisal processes are further advanced, I will be in a better position to provide further details on individual projects.Partnership delivery is key to the provision of high-quality active travel networks. Combining direct departmental investment with Council led greenways is a clear demonstration of this.A good recent example of this partnership delivery would my recent announcement of progress on six major Active Travel Signature Schemes, including the Newry Inner City Greenway, which aims to connect the recently completed Carlingford Lough Greenway to the Newry Canal Towpath.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Connected Hubs project.
answer_text: The Department of Rural Community & Community Development and the Gaeltacht (DRCDG) in the Republic of Ireland manages and funds the Connected Hubs initiative.  In 2023, DRCDG commissioned a Feasibility Study to examine the potential of extending the connectedhubs.ie platform to include remote working hubs in Northern Ireland.During 2024, my officials engaged directly with consultants appointed by DRCDG and facilitated discussions with rural stakeholders to inform the study. They will continue to seek updates regarding the progress of the Feasibility Study from their counterparts in DRCDG .

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the provision and rollout of multidisciplinary teams in Fermanagh, including current staffing levels, service availability and any plans to address gaps in access for patients.
answer_text: The MDT Programme Team in SPPG are working closely with the WHSCT and South-West Federation to progress the recruitment of MDT staff.Transformation funding of £549,755 was allocated to the South West Federation on 10 October 2025, as per my MDT Implementation Plan for Phase 1.  To address gaps in access for patients the South-West Federation have confirmed that a timeline for recruitment for MDT physiotherapy and mental health staff as follows: -MDT RoleWTE Staff to be RecruitedRevised Phase 1 Month of RecruitmentPhysiotherapist2March 2026Physiotherapist2March 2026Mental Health Practitioner2January 2026Mental Health Practitioner2January 2026    The MDT Programme Team in SPPG are working closely with the South-West Federation and WHSCT to create and extend waiting lists and develop a recruitment plan over the next 4 years for all core roles including physiotherapists, mental health practitioners and social workers/ social work assistants. It should be noted that investment in the first year, 2025-26, will be limited to mental health practitioners and physiotherapists.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any assessment her Department has made of the regulatory support needs of credit unions, particularly those serving rural communities such as Fermanagh; and (ii) any steps being taken to ensure their long-term sustainability and capacity to provide affordable financial services.
answer_text: My Department undertook a consultation between April and June 2025 which put forward proposals for legislative and regulatory changes for credit unions, with the aim of introducing a Credit Union Bill within this current mandate. The response analysis of the consultation and next steps document will be published soon. A Rural Needs Impact Assessment (RNIA) was also carried out in conjunction with the consultation. This is a working document, which will be updated by officials during the legislative process.The regulation of credit unions is carried out by the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) and the Prudential Regulation Authority. My officials will continue to be in close contact with the two regulatory bodies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps (i) his Department; and (ii) the Western Health and Social Care Trust, plan to take to recruit 16 GPs, as well as the additional clinical and clerical staff, required for the new Lisnaskea Health Centre, in order to ensure that patients have sufficient and timely access to services.
answer_text: The GMS contract for Lisnaskea Medical Centre, GP Practice, is currently held by the FSU GP Contract Management CIC, and they have the responsibility for recruiting the appropriate level of clinical and administrative staff to ensure that patients of their practice have timely access to services.  It is the ultimate responsibility of the GP Practice, as contract holder, to ensure that GMS services can be provided safely by their practice team.  The changing face of GMS provision has recently seen inclusion of non-GP members to practice teams, including General Practice Pharmacists, Physiotherapists and Advanced Nurse Practitioners.   It is anticipated that the inclusion of these team member within practice teams will lead to a change in the GP to patient ratio in many practices.  There has also been development of telephone triage and remote consultation over the past number of years to help address steadily increasing workload in GP Practice.  Many practices will supplement the work of their 'core' staff with remote GP Locum sessional support.With regards to the recruitment and retention of GPs across Northern Ireland, DoH have also commissioned an Attract, Recruit, Retain scheme to support GP Practices to attract, recruit and retain GPs into their practice team.With regards to the new health and care centre in Lisnaskea, this will provide a central hub in Lisnaskea and will enable linkages with a number of rural GP practices. The Western Health & Social Care Trust will relocate a range of services to the new Lisnaskea Health & Care Centre.  Some of these services will be based in the Centre including the staff from the District Nursing teams, Older Peoples Social Work team and Older Peoples Day Care.  There will also be a range of services that will outreach to the Centre to provide regular clinics including Physiotherapy, Speech Therapy, Dietetics and specialist nursing teams.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her plans to bring forward a public consultation on a review of speed limits.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. I intend to launch a public consultation in the new year on a number of speed related matters. The consultation will seek views on the wider use of permanent 20mph speed limits, the lowering of national speed limit on rural single and dual carriageways, and alignment of HGV speed limits with other regions.  When the consultation is launched, I would encourage as many people as possible to contribute to this significant and important piece of work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any consideration was given to expanding NISRA's scope for data capturing rather than designing a bespoke Rural Research and Evidence Model.
answer_text: NISRA is the statistics authority for Northern Ireland and has been involved in the development of the new long-term rural policy, including this proposal for a Rural Research and Evidence Model. NISRA will continue to have a key role going forward.  Subject to the appropriate approvals being in place, I hope to launch the new rural policy for public consultation in spring 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the level of fire and rescue cover currently provided in (a) Enniskillen; (b) Belleek; (c) Irvinestown; and (d) Lisnaskea, fire stations; and (ii) how his Department is ensuring that the Fire and Rescue Service has sufficient resources and personnel to provide safe and effective emergency response across Fermanagh.
answer_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail; The level of fire and rescue cover currently provided in (a) Enniskillen; (b) Belleek; (c) Irvinestown; and (d) Lisnaskea, fire stationsHow his Department is ensuring that the Fire and Rescue Service has sufficient resources and personnel to provide safe and effective emergency response across Fermanagh.ANSWERThe number of fire and rescue crew currently provided is (a) 16 (b) 12 (c) 14 and (d) 13.The Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service operates a real-time availability system that monitors On-Call crew readiness across all stations. This system is overseen by the Regional Control Centre, which actively assesses crew availability and, where shortfalls are identified, takes appropriate action to maintain effective emergency response. This may include reallocating resources from nearby stations to ensure coverage.  To support service delivery and supervisory management of an operational incident, 6 Flexi Duty Officers are attached to the Enniskillen Response Point, which covers the 4 station areas. Where the incident warrants an increased level of managerial control a Flexi Duty Officer will be mobilised to the incident to provide support and ensure a safe system of work is applied.In Belleek, resilience is further supported through a Memorandum of Understanding with Donegal Fire and Rescue Service, allowing Ballyshannon’s appliance to be mobilised when needed. The Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service also proactively manages recruitment and attrition.  Trainee firefighters have been recently appointed and are in development at all the above-mentioned stations, with a further recruitment campaign scheduled for the first quarter of 2026, which will include these stations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail Emergency Department waiting times in (i) the South West Acute Hospital; and (ii) Altnagelvin Area Hospital, broken down by patients who are (a) admitted; and (ii) discharged home after treatment, within the last year.
answer_text: Information on the median time spent in each Emergency Department (ED) from arrival to discharge or admission is published on a quarterly basis in the ‘Urgent and Emergency Care Waiting Time Statistics’ report, the most recent of which covers the period July to September 2025, and is available on my Department’s website: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/emergency-care-waiting-timesPerformance against waiting time targets remains below expectations across Northern Ireland, including within the Western Health and Social Care Trust. My Department continues to work with the Trust to address these pressures, as highlighted in the Getting it Right First Time (GIRFT) Review of Emergency Medicine.In the Western Trust, actions to improve patient flow and reduce discharge delays include:expansion of Hospital at Home services;strengthened care home protocols;increased dementia and complex care capacity;a Control Room and Discharge Co-ordination Team to support timely discharge.At Altnagelvin Area Hospital, additional measures include:a new minor injuries unit (8am–9pm daily);enhanced Ambulatory Care Unit;a Respiratory Hub and seven-day consultant cover.These initiatives aim to reduce overcrowding and improve patient experience across the Western Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what updated steps his Department has taken since the 2016 ash dieback survey to manage and mitigate the disease.
answer_text: From 2016 to 2018 DAERA continued to survey for the spread of ash dieback disease. Surveys were undertaken on the basis of 10km grid squares across Northern Ireland, and to determine if the disease was present within each grid square. Analysis of the data collected by 2018 indicated that ash dieback was then present in at least 84% of the 10km grid squares that cover Northern Ireland. On the basis of the extent of spread, including in the wider environment, DAERA developed options, and consulted with 500+ targeted stakeholders in 2018 to consider the need to manage the impact of the disease as its eradication was not possible. As a result, the following steps were implemented to manage and mitigate the disease. The cessation of Statutory Plant Health Notices requiring the destruction of infected ash trees. Legislative controls on the movement of ash and biosecurity requirements continued to apply. Scientific research aimed at identifying any tolerances within the ash tree population and tree breeding programmes has been commissioned in partnership with DAFM and DEFRA. The Department provided guidance to landowners on managing diseased ash trees from a public safety perspective, and hosted training events for councils and other public bodies. The Forest Improvement Grant supports the regeneration of woodlands affected by the disease.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of positive ash dieback infections, in each year from 2016 to 2024, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Prior to and including 2016, DAERA undertook a dedicated surveillance programme for ash dieback, due to the extent of its spread since it was first identified in 2012 in Northern Ireland. This surveillance was undertaken on a 10km grid square basis to determine the geographic areas within which ash dieback was present. Surveillance for the disease was embedded into the Department’s plant health pest and disease inspections and continued throughout 2017 and 2018. Analysis of the data collected by 2018, indicated that ash dieback was then present in at least 84% of the 10km grid squares that cover Northern Ireland and represents infection findings within each constituency. Accordingly, ash dieback surveys ceased, as it had been already determined that the disease was considered to be widespread, including in the wider environment. The findings recorded in 2016, 2017 and 2018 are 226, 39 and 270 respectively, however these represent disease presence within the 10km squares and do not indicate the numbers of infected trees therein. Consequently, the numbers of positively infected trees are not available by constituency.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps private residents have to take in order to claim compensation from Forestry Service for damage caused to their property from fallen Forestry Service-owned trees.
answer_text: Residents whose properties have been affected by storm damaged trees, including those from the Forest Service estate, should in the first instance seek advice from their insurance providers or legal representatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of patients currently being treated for endometriosis, broken down by (a) Health and Social Care Trust; and (b) hospital; and (ii) the average waiting time from initial referral to treatment, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust,
answer_text: All Trusts have indicated that it is difficult to answer this question accurately as there are many levels of endometriosis and those women with less invasive endometriosis will be seen in general gynaecology clinics. question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any actions his Department is taking to promote digital inclusion, particularly among older people.
answer_text: My department has a dedicated Digital Inclusion Unit, which promotes the use of online government services and delivers free, tailored training to help individuals build the skills, confidence, and trust needed to engage with the digital world.The Go ON NI Programme delivers sessions in local libraries, community centres, and hubs, which are targeted at groups traditionally affected by digital exclusion. Over 1400 members of the public have already availed of this support during the current financial year. During Positive Ageing Month in October, the team participated in several events to promote digital inclusion, especially for older people, and provided advice and guidance on how to stay safe online.I recently visited one of the digital training workshops in Craigavon, delivered in partnership with Supporting Communities, to witness first-hand the work to support those most vulnerable and digitally excluded within our society.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail what engagement his Department has had with the Western Health and Social Care Trust since the announcement to pause the consultation on the future of emergency general surgery; and (ii) to outline the next steps that his Department in conjunction with the Western Health and Social Care Trust will take.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) when the Education Authority's Home to School Transport Review will conclude; and (ii) when the report will be made available.
answer_text: The Department of Education (DE) holds responsibility for the Home to School Transport Policy and the Education Authority is responsible for the operation and delivery of school transport. A review of the transport policy was initiated by DE in 2018; however, this process was paused by the then Minister due to the COVID-19 pandemic and subsequently suspended pending the outcome of the Independent Review of Education.The transport policy is extensive in scope and affects a significant number of families, with over 90,000 pupils availing of free school transport each day. It underpins other departmental policies, including school attendance, and provides essential support for many pupils with Special Educational Needs and Disabilities. Any amendments to the policy criteria have the potential to substantially increase or decrease the number of pupils eligible for free transport. Therefore, any such proposals must be carefully considered within the framework of a full policy review to assess their impact on children and families and to establish the associated financial implications.I am committed to improving school transport and I want to carry out a review of the transport policy as I feel many of its outworkings can provide challenges and frustrations for parents. Currently, my officials are conducting a scoping exercise to examine key aspects of school transport where improvement is needed. The cost of delivering school transport is considerable, exceeding £120 million in the 2023/24 financial year. Given the significant budgetary constraints currently facing my Department, any policy proposals to expand eligibility criteria must be carefully evaluated for affordability. My decision on the timing of a comprehensive review of the DE transport policy will be made within the broader strategic and financial context.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans his Department has put in place to improve timeframes for parents and young people in accessing autism spectrum disorder assessments.
answer_text: I acknowledge that the current waiting times for autism spectrum disorder (ASD) are lengthy and growing for children across Northern Ireland. This situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery. I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding.On the 22 May 2025 my Department published the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework for public consultation. The Framework promotes a person centred and needs based care pathway that will allow for a holistic early offer of support to help with presenting needs. Health and Social Care Trusts across the region are implementing a range of coordinated and localised strategies to address increasing demand for autism assessments and thereby reduce waiting times. These actions include:Trusts are reviewing and refining assessment pathways to reduce clinical time per case while maintaining quality. Assessments are aligned with NICE guidelines to ensure consistency and clinical robustness.Non-recurring investments are used to purchase additional assessments where possible. Independent sector providers are being contracted to carry out diagnostic assessments as part of waiting list initiatives.The Emotional Health and Wellbeing referral form is used regionally to ensure appropriate referrals and reduce unnecessary delays. Early triage ensures children are placed on the correct diagnostic pathway from the outset.Staff job plans are structured to optimise clinical capacity. Vacant posts are filled promptly, and recruitment is prioritised to maintain service continuity. Additional investment in specialist clinical staff is recognised as essential to meet growing demand.Services are adapting to increasing complexity, with more children presenting with comorbid ASD and ADHD, requiring more intensive assessment. Efforts are made to deliver the maximum number of comprehensive assessments within available resources.Trusts actively participate in regional forums to address demand-capacity challenges and standardise pathways.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) an update on the introduction of new public realms schemes; and (ii) if Lisnaskea would be eligible for scheme inclusion.
answer_text: My Department continuously considers a range of public realm priorities. These schemes are developed in collaboration with key stakeholders, including local Councils and the Department for Infrastructure (DfI), and are progressed through the appropriate business case and statutory approval processes.The Department for Communities regeneration policy is focussed on urban settlements with populations of 5,000 or more.  As Lisnaskea has a population below this threshold, it falls outside the scope of my Department’s urban regeneration policy remit.  However, my Department is currently developing a new regeneration initiative (Shaping Sustainable Places programme) in collaboration with the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) and the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA). Subject to securing the necessary business case approval, it is envisaged that this programme will support settlements with populations below the 5,000 threshold.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) what steps he is taking to ensure that regeneration programmes are balanced between urban and rural areas; and (ii) what engagement he has had with the Minister for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs in this respect.
answer_text: My Department’s regeneration policy focus is on urban settlements with a population of 5,000 or more and, therefore, I do not have responsibility for rural regeneration programmes. However, my Department is currently working with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs and the Department for Infrastructure, on the Shaping Sustainable Places programme, which is a long term, community-led approach to regenerating both urban and rural centres and will have a role in striking a balance between both those areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department is taking to attract, recruit and retain GPs in rural areas.
answer_text: I recognise the pressures facing General Practice and I am also very aware of the challenges in attracting and retaining GPs, particularly in rural areas. 		Against the background of a challenging financial climate, my Department has continued its focus on building the workforce and has funded a number of initiatives to recruit and retain GPs.  		The Attract, Recruit, Retain scheme supports recruitment of GPs. Since its establishment in January 2023, 101 practices have applied and received approval in principle for funding under the scheme with 61 practices having received final approval for payment in respect of a recruited GP.		The GP retainer scheme offers a flexible and supportive framework for experienced GPs who wish to continue practising without committing to full-time hours. This approach has been instrumental in retaining skilled professionals who might otherwise have left the profession due to work life balance considerations.		The GP Mentor Scheme enables GPs who have been working in General Practice in NI for some time, as well as those entering the workforce, to avail of support and mentorship from experienced, trained peers in a safe and positive environment.		GP Fellowship Schemes are supporting recently qualified GPs to transition from the training environment to independent practice, with opportunities for personal and professional development.		The Northern Ireland Induction and Refresher scheme has been updated and is now known as the International Induction Programme (IIP) and the Return to Practice Programme (RtP).  The IIP provides a structured and supportive route for internationally trained GPs to enter General Practice in Northern Ireland.  The updated programme aligns to the General Medical Council’s new pathways and includes a streamlined process for GPs who qualified in the Republic of Ireland.		The RtP programme supports UK trained GPs who have been out of practice for over two years in refreshing their skills and knowledge, ensuring they can return to independent practice with confidence.  The updated programme includes changes to streamline the application process and to reflect current best practice across the UK.		My Department has increased the annual number of GP training places from 65 in 2015/16 to the current all-time high level of 121.  Furthermore, the GP Training Group, which includes the NI Medical and Dental Training Agency (NIMDTA) and GP representatives including the Royal College of General Practitioners Northern Ireland (RCGPNI), will also progress work to project the number of GP training places required over the next 5 years.		My Department has worked with key stakeholders, to increase the exposure of undergraduates to General Practice; encourage more GP trainees; and ensure that there are sufficient GP training practices to meet demand and support GPs in training. 		The Graduate Entry Programme at the Ulster University’s Medical School is a significant development in the provision of medical educ

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for an update on the Rural Policy Framework; and (ii) to detail how it will support sustainable rural communities.
answer_text: Earlier this year, I sought support from my Executive colleagues to develop a new rural policy for Northern Ireland.  In May, my Department embarked on a co-design process with a wide range of stakeholders across central and local government and key rural stakeholders.  Three Working Groups were established with the aim of developing draft policy proposals to support, understand and champion our rural communities. I am pleased with the progress made by the co-design Working Groups who have been working intensively over the summer.  I intend to launch a public consultation on the new policy proposals in early 2026.  These proposals, which will require NI Executive approval, will detail plans to support, understand and champion rural communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current patient numbers within nursing and care homes in the Fermanagh and South Tyrone constituency; and (ii) the capacity of each.
answer_text: There are 468 registered Care Homes in Northern Ireland, providing a total of 15,836 beds. Nursing Home beds make up approximately two thirds of these beds, with one third being within Residential Care Homes. Independent sector Care Homes account for 93% of the total Care Home beds registered in Northern Ireland.The constituency of Fermanagh and South Tyrone is covered by the Western Health and Social Care Trust and the Southern Health and Social Care Trust. Across the region the Western and Southern Trust areas have the least number of registered care home beds, in comparison to the other Trusts. The Western Health and Social Care Trust has 31 nursing homes and 36 residential care homes with a total of 2079 care home beds. The Southern Health and Social Care Trust has 48 nursing home beds and 60 residential care homes with a total of 2568 beds. (Census of Bed Availability in Registered Care Homes in Northern Ireland on 27 September 2023 (Dec 2023)).In the Western Health and Social Care Trust, as of 07/08/2025, there are 5 vacant available beds. None of these are located within Fermanagh and South Tyrone Constituency.In the Southern Health and Social Care Trust, as of 07/08/2025, there are 27 vacant available beds. Only 2 of these are located within Fermanagh and South Tyrone Constituency.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide an update on the work of the Northern Ireland Bureaux.
answer_text: Through our Programme for Government, we have set out an ambitious programme of change. Our overseas offices play a key role in its delivery.In the past year, in addition to providing early insights into emerging policy and market developments across the world’s largest economies, our Bureaux have supported multiple Ministerial, senior official and stakeholder visits to the US, Canada, Brussels and Singapore.They have also hosted numerous events and promoted our key messages across Europe, North America and China – ensuring that we are best placed to avail of economic and educational opportunities, influence developing policy and offset challenges faced by emerging global issues, such as climate change and the introduction of US tariffs. Looking forward, our Bureaux are focused on delivery of our Programme for Government priorities by making crucial connections between our sectoral leaders and their global counterparts, building increasing awareness of our talents and strengths, and seeking opportunities to develop relationships with emerging leaders and markets.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of deliveries carried out in the (i) South West Acute Hospital Maternity Unit; and (ii) Altnagelvin Area Hospital Maternity Unit in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The number of births while admitted to, or en route and immediately prior to admission to, a Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust hospital, by hospital, are included in the ‘Northern Ireland Inpatient Activity Statistics’ publication. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how he plans to ensure rural pre-school settings have flexible Published Admission Number allocation to guarantee children are placed in their locality.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA), in cooperation with the EA Pre-School Education Group, is responsible for setting Pupil Allocation Numbers for non-statutory pre-school settings.  The EA wrote to providers on 7 February to set out the process for the allocation of initial PANs, as well as the process followed in reviewing those allocations. This process includes specific consideration of rural pre-school settings. Each year, at least 99.8% children receive the offer of a funded place in a pre-school setting of their parents’ preference by the end of stage two of the pre-school admissions process.  We are currently at the beginning of the second stage of the admissions process for the 2025-26 academic year.  To date, 97% of children have already received the offer of a funded place in a setting of their parents’ preference. Should additional funded places be required in local areas, the EA will adjust allocations to settings where necessary.Nonetheless, I have requested that the EA review the PAN allocation process so that opportunities for improvement can be identified and explored.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide an update on what work is being done in relation to racial equality post-2025.
answer_text: Work to develop a new post-2025 Racial Equality Strategy continues in partnership with communities. We are committed to eliminating racism and racial inequality. We have taken learning from other programmes which deal with societal issues and believe a strong focus on a public health approach is needed if we are to make a real difference. It is important that we listen to the views and experiences of those most impacted, that is why we launched a public call for views to give everyone a chance to have their say. We will reflect on the feedback and will continue to engage with key stakeholders, including the Racial Equality Subgroup and the wider Minority Ethnic sector, in the development process.

The report also concludes that a more targeted approach is needed toensure that the correct safety intervention is selected and work updating our policy documentis underway. Funding for progressing the next tranche of part-time 20 mph speed limits atschools will be included within budget planning considerations for next financial year.I would also like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for roadsafety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener. As part of my consideration of therecently published review I have asked my officials to prepare a paper on 20mph speed limitsin general taking into consideration schools, residential areas and places where there is a highernumber of those walking, wheeling and cycling.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the work her Department is undertaking in relation to Community Wealth Building.
answer_text: Question 8 AQO 1592/22-27 grouped with Question 13 AQO 1597/22-27My Department continues to make positive progress around Community Wealth Building and the recommendations in the 2022 report.  Last week, I met with the Ministers for Finance and Communities to discuss our ongoing progress in advance of meeting with Development Trusts NI.I was delighted to attend a meeting to learn about the work the Larne Community Wealth Building initiative has undertaken, and to hear directly from members about their future plans. I was encouraged by the collaborative approach, not only between the anchor organisations, but also the shared learning between Larne and the North West Community Wealth Building Partnerships.One of the recommendations from the 2022 report asked the department to “explore the potential for cooperatives, employee ownership, and worker buyouts.”In August my department published research on the benefits, and potential growth, of employee ownership models. Employee Ownership Ireland was recently established to support businesses exploring employee ownership as a future business model. I will be meeting representatives from this organisation shortly to discuss how we can support this work further.  I attended the “Co-operation among Co-operators” conference last week, held in Boundary Brewery, a local co-operative. I spoke about the important role these business models can play in delivering my economic priorities. I was pleased that my department could provide bursaries to ensure the conference was accessible for everyone, and I am excited to see where the conversations that began there will lead. Community energy is a sector where there is potential for a cooperative-type model. DfE officials are part of the Sustainable Energy Communities project advisory panel, led by Advice NI. My predecessor commissioned the Centre of Advanced Sustainable Energy (CASE) to undertake a Community Decarbonisation Pathfinder Project. This work is in the early stages of development with the key stakeholders meeting monthly to develop the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on funding for rural roads.
answer_text: I recognise there has been historical under-investment in our road network for a significant number of years and that many rural roads are in need of repairs. I am well aware of the frustrations and, at times, inconvenience this has caused. People and communities rely on the road network to do business, go to work, school, and connect to one another.The rural road network makes up around 75% of the total road network in the North and therefore a significant proportion of the overall budget allocated for Structural Maintenance is spent on the rural road network.There is a rolling programme of resurfacing across the road network, including rural roads, and my Department is working hard to ensure that available funding is targeted at areas of greatest need. For a number of years now, my Department has allocated budget to a Roads Recovery Fund, which is primarily targeted towards the lower tier network. These improvements target short lengths of mainly rural roads in particularly poor condition. I am keen to invest in our rural road network and am pleased to inform you that, in 2024/25, some £10m has been allocated to the Roads Recovery Fund, of which £8m has been directly targeted towards rural roads. This allocation will enable the repair of many short lengths of road, some in particularly poor condition, together with a number of longer resurfacing schemes, where appropriate. My officials estimate that, upon completion of the 2024/25 programme, over 400 locations on the road network will have benefitted from this initiative.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update of the review of the 20mph speed limit schemes for schools.
answer_text: A review of the initiative introducing part-time 20mph speed limits at schools has been undertaken and I recently discussed the review report with my officials.  I have asked for the report to be shared with the Infrastructure Committee, following which it will be made publicly available.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe.  The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on proposed changes to rating policy.
answer_text: When I was last in the Chamber for Oral Questions on 14 October, I highlighted my short, medium and long-term proposals for the rating system were with the Executive for consideration. That remains the case.I had hoped by now that I would have secured Executive agreement to my proposals and as a result would have been in a position to update the House on the way forward.Every pound we are spending on rate relief means reduced revenue for vital public services or higher rates bills for other homes and businesses. In that context we must make sure every rate support measure is achieving the desired policy outcomes and is delivering for our citizens and businesses. That is why the proposals I have put forward outline my intent to look at every rate support measure legislated for by the Executive. It is important to stress that review does not mean removal.  I intend to begin first and foremost with Small Business Rate Relief given its integral importance to our economy and small local businesses. That is also timely in light of the contents of the Autumn Budget.									I am also conscious of the Member’s support in the House yesterday for the rejuvenation of our high streets and town centres. I too want to work with Executive colleagues to build on the success of my reintroduction of the enhanced Back in Business scheme and look at how we help tackle the blight of vacant properties in our towns and cities in conjunction with other Executive Ministers and local councils.The Executive is now fast approaching a critical juncture to reach decisions on these matters to provide sufficient time to deliver changes to come into effect for April 2025. 						I hope therefore that the Executive can come to a decision quickly on my proposed approach for the short, medium and long term. This will enable me to get on with the job of work to make positive reforms and changes to our rating system which are reflective of the economic environment and which drive economic growth in the overall tax base.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of bovine TB cases recorded by each DAERA Direct Regional Office in each of the last three years; and (ii) the areas covered by these cases.
answer_text: Information on bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) is held on the basis of geographical regions covered by the ten Divisional Veterinary Office (DVO) boundaries rather than the 12 DAERA Direct regional offices.  Data relating to unique herd breakdowns per DVO area has been inserted in the Table 1 below.  A bTB unique herd breakdown is defined as a herd which has had at least one bTB reactor during a specified calendar year irrespective of any bTB reactors during the previous calendar year. To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether there will be a dedicated ring-fenced fund for rural communities in the north under the British Government’s Shared Prosperity Fund, similar to the Rural England Prosperity Fund.
answer_text: The Department of Finance (DoF) leads on engagement with Whitehall on directly delivered funding streams, including the Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF), with the support of Executive Departments. There are currently no ring-fenced allocations within the SPF for rural communities. However, my officials will continue to engage with and support DoF on their engagement with Whitehall on directly delivered funding streams.The Rural England Prosperity Fund is an England specific successor to EU LEADER Funding and is designed to be complementary to the SPF rather than forming part of it, funded from within the English Farm Funding Settlement rather than from the SPF budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of the commitment that the north would not have reduced funding as a result of Brexit, to detail his engagement with the British Government in relation to a ring-fenced budget for rural development.
answer_text: I have written to the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs on a number of occasions expressing my concerns about the future for the earmarked agricultural budget for Agriculture, Agri-Environment and Wider Rural economy, with the current funding package coming to a conclusion at the end of March next year. I have stressed to him that any future settlement should, as an absolute minimum, maintain the current budget allocation uplifted for inflation to allow delivery of my Department’s policy objectives and ambitions. Most recently we discussed this in person at the Inter-Ministerial Group for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs meeting in London on 16 September and following that meeting I wrote again to stress the importance of receiving early clarity on future earmarked Agricultural Support funding for next year and beyond. In my letter I also highlighted the significant contribution that the agriculture sector in Northern Ireland makes to UK food security.I have also already stressed to the Secretaries of State for Northern Ireland and for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs the need for funding decisions to reflect the importance of just transition and also made the case that there should be a separate, specific and additional capital fund also to support just transition in agriculture.  My Department is engaging with the Department of Finance on raising this with HMT as part of the broader budgetary processes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 11974/22-27, whether the six recruited consultant surgeons will be deployed as part of plans to reinstate emergency general surgery at the South West Acute Hospital.
answer_text: The Western HSC Trust is moving towards recruiting eleven whole time equivalent general surgeons.  The consultant general surgeons recruited to date have been appointed to Trust-wide contracts and are contracted to work across all sites, including the South West Acute Hospital (SWAH).  All consultants will be part of Trust-wide job planning, which will include sessions in the Elective Overnight Stay Centre in the SWAH, in Omagh Hospital and Primary Care Complex theatres and in Altnagelvin Hospital.In November 2022, the Western HSC Trust made the decision to suspend Emergency General Surgery in the SWAH for patient safety reasons.  This was due to consultant workforce pressures and inability to sustain the Emergency General Surgery rota.   In line with Change and Withdrawal of Services Guidance, the Western HSC Trust is considering the future position of Emergency General Surgery across the Trust.  The Western HSC Trust, supported by my Department, remains committed to the long-term sustainability and development of the SWAH.  This includes building elective capacity, developing a safe and sustainable model for general surgery across the Trust, working collaboratively at local and regional level to identify opportunities for strategic development and ensuring a sustainable workforce.

To ask the Minister of Education for an update in relation to the new capital build for Bunscoil an Traonaigh, Lisnaskea.
answer_text: The relocation of Bunscoil an Traonaigh to the former Lisnaskea High School site is being progressed by the Department as a Minor Works scheme. The business case for the relocation is currently being reviewed by the Department. Following business case approval, the works will be subject to capital availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he is considering the reopening of the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive.
answer_text: Following the success of the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme (RoIRS) which was based on the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive and introduced in June 2021, a bid for funding for a replacement scheme was submitted to the Department of Finance during this year’s budget setting process. It was intended that a new reimbursement scheme could have treated over 4,000 patients this financial year. Unfortunately, under the 2024/25 Budget recently approved by the Executive, precisely zero additional funding has been provided for tackling our waiting lists.Unfortunately, instead of now being able to invest in such waiting list initiatives, my Department’s budget has been cut by over 2%. This is a deeply regrettable and concerning position.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department's plans to address waiting lists for breast reconstruction surgeries.
answer_text: Breast reconstruction surgery, when reconstruction is an option following a mastectomy, either partial or full, is now an integral component of the treatment process for breast cancer.Not only does reconstructive surgery aim to rebuild the physical appearance, it also aims to improve body image and self-esteem of the person living with a cancer diagnosis.  This can also contribute to the process of recovery on a physical, emotional and psychological level.Each Health and Social Care Trust had an opportunity to submit funding bids to the Strategic Planning and Performance Group Performance, in relation to the Waiting List Initiative funding.  This is part of the Elective Care Framework, launched in June 2021, which includes elective breast reconstruction surgery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on his Department's efforts to address MOT delays.
answer_text: In each of the last two years the DVA has conducted over 1.1 million vehicle tests which are the highest numbers ever recorded.  This increase in capacity has been achieved through a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, with the latest tranche of new examiners due to be posted to test centres from mid/end June onwards.The DVA also use overtime to provide cover for leave and offer vehicle test appointments on Sundays and bank holidays when testing is not normally available. In 2023, there were only seven days in the year when they did not offer MOT appointments.  Disappointingly, in the region of 900 customers per week fail to attend a booked appointment, which places additional demand on this service.From 1 June 2024, private cars first registered between 1 June 2019 and 31 May 2020 or between 1 June 2017 and 31 May 2018, which have a valid MOT issued by the DVA will have a one-year Temporary Exemption Certificate or TEC automatically applied from the date their current MOT certificate expires. Vehicle licensing information shows that around 115,000 cars may be eligible for a TEC.I am confident that the introduction of TECs will provide relief for many customers and will gradually improve waiting times over the course of the implementation period, with DVA targeting an improvement to around six weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what support her Department will provide to BT/ EE staff following the announcement of 300 job losses in Enniskillen.
answer_text: Oral Question No 13 – AQO 507/22-27I personally met with the BT/EE to impress the importance of helping those that do not wish to voluntarily leave find other employment. While over 80% of the BT/EE staff accepted the Leavers package, and others are transferring to BT’s Headquarters in Belfast, a number of workers will be looking for alternative employment.My department, and officials from Invest NI, will work with BT/EE, along with other local stakeholders, to support those staff impacted by this regrettable announcement. We will engage with Department for Communities Jobs and Benefits, Fermanagh and Omagh District Council, Enterprise Northern Ireland, Local Enterprise Agency and other local businesses and stakeholders, to provide advice and identify opportunities for alternative employment, self-employment and/or re-training opportunities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 9909/22-27, and as the information requested was provided in March, whether the Departmental Solicitor’s Office has carried out their legal due diligence process to allow for completion of the business case.
answer_text: For clarification, the business case for Carrowshee Park/Sylvan Hill Community Association was approved in March 2020, with a business case addendum approved in February 2022. The Departmental Solicitor’s Office (DSO) has confirmed that not all the requested information has been received.  DSO has written to the Community Group’s solicitor on 19 March and again on 16 April, summarising the legal issues that remain outstanding. However, to date no further response has been received. Until this information has been received, and the legal due diligence process completed, the scheme cannot progress. As I previously advised, progression of the scheme is also subject to budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 6738/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the stakeholders he has engaged with in relation to the new Rural Development Programme.
answer_text: I acknowledge that continued support for farmers and rural dwellers following the closure of the 2014-2020 Northern Ireland Rural Development Programme has been a key concern for our rural stakeholders.	A new framework for supporting farmers is well advanced and stakeholder engagement and co-design are core principles of this ongoing programme. In respect of rural policy focused on rural dwellers and communities, I, along with my Department Officials, have met with various stakeholders concerned about future rural policy and I remain committed to continuing my engagement with them as work continues to set the direction of future rural policy post EU exit. Following receipt of a number of further meeting invitations from a range of rural stakeholders I also plan a round table meeting with them in the not-too-distant future to strive to achieve the best possible outcome for all rural dwellers. 	As you are aware the Rural Needs Act (NI) 2016 places a duty on public authorities to have due regard to the social and economic needs of people in rural areas when exercising their functions and my department continues to provide support to public authorities to help ensure that the Act is implemented effectively.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has a workforce plan in relation to recruiting and retaining surgical staff in the South West Acute Hospital.
answer_text: Given challenges in the attraction, recruitment, and retention of medical staff across all its hospitals, medical workforce stabilisation, including surgery at SWAH, is a priority for the Western HSC Trust.To address these challenges a Medical Workforce Stabilisation project has been in place since early 2023 with a remit to proactively address attraction, recruitment, and retention of medical staff.  In addition, the Trust’s Chief Executive chairs a Programme Board for the review of general surgery, which has overall responsibility for the implementation of a new model of general surgical services within the Trust.  This board provides the strategic direction and coordination to guide and support the reform and modernisation process.A range of strategies aimed at attracting, recruiting, and retaining the medical workforce are also being progressed by the Trust.  This includes tailored recruitment material promoting the West as a great place to live and work. Targeted recruitment is undertaken based on funded posts and identified service needs, with vacancies promoted on an ongoing, worldwide basis, using a streamlined recruitment and application process.  Flexible working arrangements and working longer guidance support work life balance and encourage longer working lives, with new roles available to support medical staff.  A Retire and Return policy is also in place which allows retired doctors to temporarily support services.  International medical recruits have a defined support and development programme, with significant pastoral and social support.  Temporary accommodation is available to facilitate new arrivals and their families.Consultant general surgeons are recruited to Trust-wide contracts, with appointees contracted to work across all sites, including SWAH.  Since February 2023, six substantive consultant surgeons (including two international recruits) have been appointed, all of whom are due to take up posts in the coming months.  Two additional retired consultant surgeons have been attracted on part time “as and when” contracts.The Trust continues to recruit general surgeons at middle grade level to SWAH.  These staff support assessment of surgical problems at the Emergency Department and also deliver ambulatory care services.  Local GPs also have direct access to this service for same or next day assessment or advice.  Four physician associates have also been appointed to SWAH.  My Department supplements the Trust’s operational workforce planning by leading a long-term, regional strategic workforce planning programme which is aligned to, and an enabler of, the Transformation agenda.  While concentrating on workforce supply, it also considers opportunities for workforce development.  Medical specialties, including surgical specialties, are part of this strategic workforce planning programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps his Department is taking to recruit and retain GPs in rural areas.
answer_text: I recognise the increasing pressures facing General Practice, particularly in rural areas.  I have been clear that stabilising GP services is a key priority for me and one which my Department is pro-actively taking steps to address.  I welcome the recent agreement of the 2024/25 General Medical Services (GMS) contract which is an important step forward to achieving greater stability in General Practice.   The contract makes significant progress against key aims identified by GPs in their negotiations, including provision of dedicated funding for indemnity costs.  	My Department has been working with Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts and GP Federations to promote stability in vulnerable, rural areas.  Despite the number of contract hand backs, through working with key stakeholders including HSC Trusts and GP Federations, it has been possible to ensure that no practice has closed and that patients can continue to access GP services in their areas.  Against the background of a challenging financial climate, my Department continues its focus on building the workforce.  This includes funding the Attract, Recruit, Retain Scheme, the GP Retainer Scheme and the GP Induction and Refresher Scheme and increasing the number of GP training places to its current level of 121.  My Department will also be working with the NI Medical and Dental Training Agency (NIMDTA) to undertake a review of the GP training programme to consider innovations which will enhance the recruitment and retention of GP trainees in Northern Ireland.  The Graduate Entry Programme at the Ulster University’s Medical School is a significant development in the provision of medical education in Northern Ireland.  The curriculum places an emphasis on primary care placements, with a high concentration of clinical placements in rural settings in the west and north.  My Department has provided significant financial support to help stabilise primary care services.  Regrettably, the 2024-25 Budget outcome was significantly less than what my Department required and has resulted in 2.3% less funding in 2024-25 than we spent in 2023-24.  This means that Health will be facing significant budgetary pressures in 2024-25 with some extremely difficult decisions required to manage within the budget allocated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the consultation on the Northern Ireland Food Strategy Framework.
answer_text: My officials have been leading on the development of a cross-departmental Northern Ireland Food Strategy Framework. The Framework proposes a new strategic collaborative approach around food that will help to improve economic, environmental, health and social outcomes for Northern Ireland. In recommending an innovative food systems approach, the draft Framework provides an opportunity to optimise cross-departmental working on food matters. This will help to deliver departmental and future Programme for Government objectives, addressing health and wellbeing, the environment and food poverty, as well as regional economic prosperity.An eight-week public consultation exercise was carried out in Autumn 2021 to seek views on the proposed vision, principles and goals of a Northern Ireland Food Strategy Framework. Overall, the responses indicated a strong endorsement for the proposed Framework approach. A summary of the responses is available on the DAERA website.A Review Lab involving all relevant Departments, Food Standards Agency and Invest NI was held in October 2023 with the aim of testing the Draft Food Strategy Framework against recent changes in the strategic and policy context and refreshing the text as required. In the light of the feedback received, an updated draft Framework has been developed. I have reviewed the draft Framework and intend to discuss with Executive colleagues before consideration of its publication.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the impact of bank closures on rural communities.
answer_text: I am deeply concerned about the impact of bank branch closures on our towns, villages and rural communities.  Many citizens and small businesses will now have further to travel to get to their closest branch, as a result of ongoing closures. This may not be easy or even possible for some of our most vulnerable. Staff employed in branches will also be concerned about the risk of job losses.That is why I held a roundtable meeting with senior representatives from the local banks last week. I used that meeting to press on them the need to protect the branch network and maintain access to vital banking services here, particularly for our most vulnerable.  I also emphasised the importance of quickly rolling out further banking hubs in those areas left without bank branches.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update in relation to the new Carrowshee Park / Sylvan Hill Community Association (Carrosyl), Lisnaskea project.
answer_text: Following Dr Mark Browne’s update to you on 8 January 2024, the Department is still in the process of completing the legal due diligence process for Carrowshee Park/Sylvan Hill, which is required to enable the scheme to progress, subject to funding being available. The Departmental Solicitor’s Office (DSO) have provided the youth group with itemised lists of the outstanding information and documentation required to complete this process on a number of occasions. Until all the requested information has been received, and the legal due diligence process completed, the scheme is not able to progress any further.
